rationale: The page contains detailed information on the applications and global reserves of Rare Earth Elements (REE), as well as a comprehensive overview of Nanotechnology, including its definition, types, and key applications across various sectors. This provides multiple factual points suitable for question generation.
cleanedText:
Potential Applications of Rare Earth Elements (REE):
- Electronics and Electricals: E.g., used in television screens and computer monitors because many REE have phosphorescent properties.
- Renewable Energy and Green Technologies: REE alloys prevent corrosion in metals and increase efficiency in energy storage.
- Rare Earth Magnets: REE magnets have high remanence and coercivity.
- Other: Defence (e.g., radars), industrial catalyst, etc.
- China dominates with more than 2/3rd of global production and controls more than 85% of the global supply chain.
World Reserves of Rare Earth Elements, 2024:
- China: 49%
- Brazil: 23%
- India: 8%
- Australia: 6%
- Russia: 4%
- Others: 10%
Distribution of REE in India:
- India’s REE reserves are mostly found in monazite sands.
- Coastal states have the most rare earth deposits, namely Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh, and Odisha. (2023 Indian Minerals Yearbook).
8.3. NANOTECHNOLOGY
Concept Corner: Nanotechnology
- Involves working with materials and devices at the nanoscale, typically ranging from 1 to 100 nm in size (nanoparticle).
- On the nanometer scale, materials may exhibit unusual properties. Their properties depend on shape, size, surface characteristics, and inner structure.
- Types:
- Natural nanomaterials: E.g. Volcanic ash
- Artificial nanomaterials: E.g. Carbon Nanotube
- Concern/Challenge: Potential toxicity of nanoparticles to humans and the environment.
Key Applications of Nanotechnology:
- Agriculture: Nanofertilizers (nutrient uptake with 90-100% utilization efficiency), Nanobiosensors (Monitor soil conditions), etc.
- Health: Better imaging and diagnostics, Targeted Drug Delivery, etc.
- Energy: Nanostructured solar cells could be cheaper to manufacture and easier to install, etc.
- Environment: Nanomaterials can be employed in water purification and desalination technologies etc.
- Electronics and IT: Quantum dots and other nanostructures can be used to develop advanced displays, lighting, etc.
- Other: Cosmetics (uses nanoparticles of some metallic oxides), etc.
